title: The Image of God Is the Key, the Incarnation of God Is the Engine
author: James Silberman
orientation: Silberman argues that the secular pro-life argument is insufficient because abortion supporters now openly concede the humanity of the preborn while denying intrinsic moral value. Only the doctrine of the image of God and the incarnation of Christ supplies the foundation needed to refute the humanist worldview behind abortion.
audiences: Mainstream pro-life leader uncomfortable with explicit Christianity
Pro-choice reader who acknowledges fetal humanity
Pastor or church leader weighing how to address abortion

questions.question: Why does the author insist the abolitionist movement must be explicitly Christian rather than a religiously neutral coalition?
questions.answer: Because abolitionists believe Christianity is the foundation of the case against abortion, and they view the pro-life movement's avoidance of explicit Christian framing as a strategic and theological error.
questions.quote: The Abolitionist Movement is explicitly Christian. Abolitionists believe that it should be an explicitly Christian movement and we think the Pro-Life Movement has erred in not being explicitly Christian.

questions.question: What concrete example does Silberman give of a pro-life organization actively suppressing the Gospel in its outreach?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing how to address abortion
questions.answer: He cites a prominent pro-life public outreach organization that requires its volunteers to sign a form pledging not to share the Gospel unless the person they are engaging brings up religion first.
questions.quote: As an especially egregious example, one prominent pro-life public outreach organization makes its volunteer activists sign a form establishing that they won't share the Gospel with people unless the person they're talking to brings up religion first.

questions.question: If the secular pro-life argument is correct that killing innocent humans is wrong, why does Silberman call it insufficient?
questions.answer: Because the foundational claim of secular humanism is that humans have no intrinsic moral value, and a secular pro-life argument leaves that foundation untouched, refuting only a surface-level claim while conceding the underlying worldview.
questions.quote: The foundation of the secular humanist position on abortion – that human beings do not have intrinsic moral value – is left untouched by secular pro-life arguments, as these arguments fail to get at the foundation.

questions.question: What outdated assumption about abortion supporters does Silberman say has been falsified by years of public outreach?
questions.audience: Mainstream pro-life leader uncomfortable with explicit Christianity
questions.answer: The assumption that once people acknowledged the humanity of the preborn child they would oppose abortion. In reality, most abortion supporters now concede the child's humanity but still defend the mother's right to kill.
questions.quote: It has long been common pro-life thought that once people acknowledged the humanity of the preborn child, they would oppose abortion. This is understandable but is ultimately an outdated and falsified pro-life speculation.

questions.question: How does the author characterize the typical abortion supporter he encounters in public engagement?
questions.answer: Not as someone confused about embryology, but as someone morally comparable to a tyrant who knowingly endorses the killing of human beings.
questions.quote: Most abortion supporters are not the embryology equivalent of a flat-earther, they are the moral equivalent of Genghis Kahn.

questions.question: Which Salon writer does Silberman cite to show that some abortion supporters openly admit abortion ends a human life?
questions.audience: Pro-choice reader who acknowledges fetal humanity
questions.answer: Mary Elizabeth Williams, who concedes that life begins at conception while still defending abortion on the grounds that not all life is equal.
questions.quote: "So what if abortion ends life? I believe that life starts at conception. And it's never stopped me from being pro-choice."

questions.question: Beyond rhetorical strategy, what philosophical defect does the author identify in any moral argument that does not appeal to God's word?
questions.answer: Without an objective standard rooted in God's word, even a correct moral claim is arbitrary and rests on a foundation no firmer than the secular humanist's.
questions.quote: An argument against abortion (or any moral claim whatsoever) without reference to the objective standard by which something can adequately be called wrong is an arbitrary argument.

questions.question: According to Schaeffer and Koop, what is the only thing that can stem the tide running from abortion to infanticide and euthanasia?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing how to address abortion
questions.answer: The certainty of the absolute uniqueness and value of people, which can only be grounded in the knowledge that human beings are made in the image of God.
questions.quote: The only thing that can stem this tide is the certainty of the absolutely uniqueness and value of people. And the only thing which gives us this is the knowledge that people are made in the image of God.

questions.question: Why does Silberman insist that the battle against abortion cannot be fought in piecemeal fashion?
questions.answer: Because abortion is a manifestation of an entire worldview, secular humanism, that is opposed point-for-point by Christianity, and only a comprehensive worldview-level confrontation can address the root.
questions.quote: We have to understand that [the battle] is one total entity [secular humanism] opposed to the other total entity [Christianity].

questions.question: What twofold theological foundation does the author identify as the heart of the abolitionist movement?
questions.answer: The image of God in man as the key to the anti-abortion argument, and the incarnation of Christ as the engine that empowers the movement through God's saving and redeeming Spirit.
questions.quote: The image of God in man is the key to the anti-abortion argument, and the incarnation of God in Christ for the purpose of defeating death and redeeming all who believe is the engine of the abolitionist movement.

questions.question: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison to answer the modern claim that 'all life is not equal'?
questions.audience: Pro-choice reader who acknowledges fetal humanity
questions.answer: He invokes Garrison's appeal to Genesis, arguing that the image of God in every human being is a death-blow to all hierarchies of human worth and all claims of dominion over the lives of others.
questions.quote: "'So God created man in his own image – male and female he created them.' This is a death-blow to all claims of superiority, to all charges of inferiority, to all usurpation, to all oppressive dominion."

related.title: Jesus' Incarnation is the Death Knell to Abortion
related.reason: Develops the same incarnation argument Silberman names as the engine of abolitionism, applying Christ's enfleshment directly to the case against abortion.

related.title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Parallel argument that the pro-life framework is inadequate and that Christians must adopt an explicitly Christian abolitionist identity instead.

related.title: Biblical, Not Secular
related.reason: Restates the abolitionist commitment to a biblical rather than secular foundation, the very contrast Silberman draws against secular humanism.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Articulates the movement-level distinction between abolitionism and pro-life activism that Silberman defends throughout the article.

related.title: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
related.reason: Another freethestates.org piece grounding the case against abortion in Scripture rather than secular reasoning, complementing Silberman's worldview argument.

related.title: Christians could force Trump to be an abolitionist of abortion with merely a lift of the finger. #YouDontGetMyVoteYET
related.reason: Applies the same conviction that Christians, not pragmatists, must drive the fight against abortion, extending Silberman's worldview-first argument into electoral terrain.
